channel.title: SoundsBrown
channel.copyright: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International License.

item.title: MIDI
item.description: Wiring

item.title: 4046 VCO
item.description: VCO based on a 4046 IC. https://hackaday.com/2015/08/07/logic-noise-4046-voltage-controlled-oscillator-part-one/ TODO: Pitch is wildly unstable, buffer the CV input? See https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3ZLcbhSE1pc

item.title: I AM O
item.description: Passive VCA / amplitude modulation thingy. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m_z0NfnLqRM

item.title: Clock Source
item.description: Arduino-based quad clock pulse generator. The topmost knob sets the master tempo in BPM. The other three channels are derived from the master with a multiplier that can range from 1⁄16 to 16. The settings for each channel are shown on an I2C OLED display. Output for each channel is a 5V square wave with 50% duty cycle.

item.title: WAV Trigger
item.description: Robertsonics WAV Trigger sampler. SD card stores 16 sound samples. The 16 trigger pins are broken out to jacks on the front panel, so the samples can be fired with CV. The left and right channels of the output are broken out as separate mono jacks, so you could in theory load two completely different sounds in the left and right channels of each WAV file and process them independently in other modules.

item.title: Baby10 Sequencer
item.description: Classic “Baby 10” sequencer based on 4017 decade counter IC. Uses external clock signal for timing. Each step has a pot for setting the output voltage (0-5V), a gate output and an indicator LED. Global CV jack outputs the sequenced voltages. Buttons allow manual stepping and resetting to step 0. https://hackaday.com/2016/01/14/oh-baby-baby10-build-a-classic-analog-music-sequencer/ Very laborious build due to large number of front panel components, would not make again. The muxer sequencer is at least as useful in hindsight and was much easier to build.

item.title: White Noise
item.description: Dual 8-bit digital noise. PWM outputs from an Arduino are passed through an RC filter.

item.title: Attenuator
item.description: Quad attenuator: potentiometer as voltage divider + TL084 buffer. If inputs are not connected, they default to a constant 5V.

item.title: FM Radio
item.description: FM radio module based on a TEA5767 module (connected to an Arduino via I2C). Tuner frequency can be controlled by CV or by the knob on the front panel. Current frequency is shown on an Adafruit I2C LED backpack. The antenna is also broken out to the front panel. References: https://github.com/simonmonk/arduino_TEA5767 https://www.adafruit.com/product/880 TODO Output volume is too low https://github.com/mathertel/Radio seems more feature-rich than the library I used; is it worth switching?

item.title: Evil Eye
item.description: CV source based on a Sharp GP2Y0A60SZLF analog distance sensor. Move your hand (or anything else) in front of the sensor to produce 0-5V output. Can be used to create theremin-esque effects. https://www.pololu.com/product/2474

item.title: 40106 Hex Oscillator
item.description: 6 square wave oscillators with frequency knobs and sync inputs. Very common circuit based on 40106 IC. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DtUCzzjHhgM

item.title: LFO
item.description: Arduino-based digital LFO. Rate from 0.01 Hz to 37 Hz using a non-linear scale. Six waveforms: saw, ramp, triangle, square, sine and value noise (Perlin-esque). PWM control for all waveforms. Unipolar output (0V - 5V) through an MCP4725 DAC (I2C interface, 12 bit resolution). Optional status display on an I2C OLED. Output is buffered using a TL08x References: https://www.sparkfun.com/products/12918 https://github.com/olikraus/U8g2_Arduino

item.title: Muxer Sequencer
item.description: This is a sequencer-ish module built around a 4051 multiplexer/demultiplexer IC. The IC is basically an 8-way selector switch which connects the COM jack to one of the 8 in/out jacks. The selection is done by setting 3 address bits. The IC works in both directions, so you can send 1 input to 8 alternating outputs, or take 1 output from 8 alternating inputs. The module is driven by an Arduino, which consumes the clock input and manages the address lines (each clock pulse advances the sequence to the next step).

item.title: Vactrols
item.description: https://sound-au.com/project200.htm

item.title: Atari Punk Console
item.description: Atari Punk Console with CV mods. I also slapped vactrols on the two pots. References: https://sdiy.info/wiki/Atari_Punk_Console https://www.instructables.com/Voltage-Controlable-Atari-Punk-Console/

item.title: Power
item.description: Case contains an ATX power supply. Front panel breaks out the on/off switch, the standby and power on LEDs, and all the different positive voltages so it can be used like a bench supply. References: https://www.electronics-tutorials.ws/blog/convert-atx-psu-to-bench-supply.html Power connectors The backside contains bus boards that distribute +/-12V, +/-5V and GND using 5x2 pin headers and ribbon cables. TODO: Take a picture of the back.

item.title: Form factor
item.description: Eurorack “spec”: http://www.doepfer.de/a100_man/a100m_e.htm Ikea RAST = 19x10.5 inch = two rows of 3U modules ATX PSU Wooden “rails”, modules are held with wood screws Front panels from mandarin crates

item.title: Burning bootloaders
item.description: In Pololu config utility: Enable VCC output Set regulator mode to 5V Insert cable the right way around: In Arduino IDE: Board: Arduino Uno Programmer: Atmel STK500 Always use “Upload using programmer” Use first of two COM ports (e.g. COM9 if it advertises 9 and 10) Burning bootloader also just works

item.title: STM32
item.description: Note! The information below may be obsolete, check here: https://www.stm32duino.com/ https://github.com/stm32duino/wiki/wiki https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=337rDuCGeYs&list=WL&index=3&t=124s HW/SW setup Add to board manager URLs: http://dan.drown.org/stm32duino/package_STM32duino_index.json Also downloaded release from https://github.com/rogerclarkmelbourne/Arduino_STM32 Unzipped into C:\Users\Tom\Documents\Arduino\hardware\Arduino_STM32-1.0.0 Executed drivers/win/install_drivers.bat Jumpers on board are both set to 0 Pinout

item.title: Arduino IDE
item.description: Installing a dark theme Clone or download https://github.com/jeffThompson/DarkArduinoTheme Backup C:\Program Files (x86)\Arduino\lib\theme Overwrite with the theme directory from DarkArduinoTheme
